text: I arrive as open-source contributor deciding whether a project is worth a first PR. My past: I have wasted weekends on inactive repositories and now look for maintainers, docs, issues, and contribution signals before investing time.
Today I want to: Find whether GitHub helps me judge repository credibility and contribution paths quickly.
My life goal is: Build a public technical reputation without wasting years on dead projects.
I have 100 life-years. Each meaningful action costs 1 month(s); each committed dollar costs 1 year(s).

label: arrival
title: GitHub · Change is constant. GitHub keeps you ahead. · GitHub

text: I see "GitHub · Change is constant. GitHub keeps you ahead. · GitHub".
I think: I see GitHub positioning itself as a place to stay ahead, but I have not seen repository-level signals yet.
My reaction: My first reaction is cautiously positive; the brand is familiar, but this screen does not yet prove it will help me avoid dead projects.
Idea: show contribution health signals early, such as recent maintainer activity, open beginner issues, and response patterns.
Life-cost decision: Spending 1 month of life to continue is reasonable if the next step gets me closer to judging whether a project is worth my first PR.
Truth assessment: My honest judgment is that GitHub is likely useful for judging project credibility, but I need repository evidence before trusting it; confidence 0.65.
